section.title: The Allotment for Judah
section.sectionRationale: Judah, the royal tribe from which Messiah will come, receives its inheritance first and largest. The chapter maps Judah's borders, tells the vivid story of Caleb taking Hebron and Othniel winning Acsah and her springs, and lists Judah's many cities in every region, closing with an honest admission: Judah could not drive out the Jebusites from Jerusalem. It is one movement, the allotment for Judah, in which God's faithfulness in giving the land meets Israel's incomplete obedience at the very city where the King will one day reign.
section.thesis: Judah, the tribe of the coming King, receives a full and detailed inheritance from the faithful hand of God, and within it faith is rewarded as Caleb and Othniel take their portions, yet the closing note that the Jebusites remained in Jerusalem quietly exposes the incompleteness of Israel's obedience, a gap only the true Son of Judah will finally close.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Judah's boundaries, verses 1 to 12; Caleb's conquest and Acsah's springs, verses 13 to 19; the cities of Judah region by region, verses 20 to 62; and the Jebusites left in Jerusalem, verse 63.

units.label: Judah's boundaries
units.note: the allotment for the tribe of Judah

verses.text: Now the allotment for the clans of the tribe of Judah extended to the border of Edom, to the Wilderness of Zin at the extreme southern boundary:
Their southern border started at the bay on the southern tip of the Salt Sea,
proceeded south of the Ascent of Akrabbim, continued on to Zin, went over to the south of Kadesh-barnea, ran past Hezron up to Addar, and curved toward Karka.
It proceeded to Azmon, joined the Brook of Egypt, and ended at the Sea. This was their southern border.
The eastern border was the Salt Sea as far as the mouth of the Jordan. The northern border started from the bay of the sea at the mouth of the Jordan,
went up to Beth-hoglah, proceeded north of Beth-arabah, and went up to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.

Then the border went up to Debir from the Valley of Achor, turning north to Gilgal, which faces the Ascent of Adummim south of the ravine. It continued along the waters of En-shemesh and came out at En-rogel.

From there the border went up the Valley of Ben-hinnom along the southern slope of the Jebusites (that is, Jerusalem) and ascended to the top of the hill that faces the Valley of Hinnom on the west, at the northern end of the Valley of Rephaim.
From the hilltop the border curved to the spring of the Waters of Nephtoah, proceeded to the cities of Mount Ephron, and then bent around toward Baalah (that is, Kiriath-jearim).
The border curled westward from Baalah to Mount Seir, ran along the northern slope of Mount Jearim (that is, Chesalon), went down to Beth-shemesh, and crossed to Timnah.
Then it went out to the northern slope of Ekron, curved toward Shikkeron, proceeded to Mount Baalah, went on to Jabneel, and ended at the Sea.
And the western border was the coastline of the Great Sea. These are the boundaries around the clans of the descendants of Judah.

units.label: Caleb's conquest and Acsah's springs
units.note: give me springs of water as well

verses.text: According to the LORD’s command to him, Joshua gave Caleb son of Jephunneh a portion among the sons of Judah, Kiriath-arba, that is, Hebron. (Arba was the forefather of Anak.)
And Caleb drove out from there the three sons of Anak, the descendants of Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak.
From there he marched against the inhabitants of Debir (formerly known as Kiriath-sepher).
And Caleb said, “To the man who strikes down Kiriath-sepher and captures it, I will give my daughter Acsah in marriage.”
So Othniel son of Caleb’s brother Kenaz captured the city, and Caleb gave his daughter Acsah to him in marriage.
One day Acsah came to Othniel and urged him to ask her father for a field. When she got off her donkey, Caleb asked her, “What do you desire?”
“Give me a {{berakhah|blessing}},” she answered. “Since you have given me land in the Negev, give me springs of water as well.” So Caleb gave her both the upper and lower springs.

units.label: The cities of Judah region by region
units.note: the inheritance of the tribe of Judah

verses.text: This is the {{nachalah|inheritance}} of the clans of the tribe of Judah.
These were the southernmost cities of the tribe of Judah in the Negev toward the border of Edom: Kabzeel, Eder, Jagur,
Kinah, Dimonah, Adadah,
Kedesh, Hazor, Ithnan,
Ziph, Telem, Bealoth,
Hazor-hadattah, Kerioth-hezron (that is, Hazor),
Amam, Shema, Moladah,
Hazar-gaddah, Heshmon, Beth-pelet,
Hazar-shual, Beersheba, Biziothiah,
Baalah, Iim, Ezem,
Eltolad, Chesil, Hormah,
Ziklag, Madmannah, Sansannah,
Lebaoth, Shilhim, Ain, and Rimmon, twenty-nine cities in all, along with their villages.
These were in the foothills: Eshtaol, Zorah, Ashnah,
Zanoah, En-gannim, Tappuah, Enam,
Jarmuth, Adullam, Socoh, Azekah,
Shaaraim, Adithaim, and Gederah (or Gederothaim), fourteen cities, along with their villages.
Zenan, Hadashah, Migdal-gad,
Dilan, Mizpeh, Joktheel,
Lachish, Bozkath, Eglon,
Cabbon, Lahmas, Chitlish,
Gederoth, Beth-dagon, Naamah, and Makkedah, sixteen cities, along with their villages.
Libnah, Ether, Ashan,
Iphtah, Ashnah, Nezib,
Keilah, Achzib, and Mareshah, nine cities, along with their villages.
Ekron, with its towns and villages;
from Ekron to the sea, all the cities near Ashdod, along with their villages;
Ashdod, with its towns and villages; Gaza, with its towns and villages, as far as the Brook of Egypt and the coastline of the Great Sea.
These were in the hill country: Shamir, Jattir, Socoh,
Dannah, Kiriath-sannah (that is, Debir),
Anab, Eshtemoh, Anim,
Goshen, Holon, and Giloh, eleven cities, along with their villages.
Arab, Dumah, Eshan,
Janim, Beth-tappuah, Aphekah,
Humtah, Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron), and Zior, nine cities, along with their villages.
Maon, Carmel, Ziph, Juttah,
Jezreel, Jokdeam, Zanoah,
Kain, Gibeah, and Timnah, ten cities, along with their villages.
Halhul, Beth-zur, Gedor,
Maarath, Beth-anoth, and Eltekon, six cities, along with their villages.
Kiriath-baal (that is, Kiriath-jearim), and Rabbah, two cities, along with their villages.
These were in the wilderness: Beth-arabah, Middin, Secacah,
Nibshan, the City of Salt, and En-gedi, six cities, along with their villages.

units.label: The Jebusites left in Jerusalem
units.note: Judah could not drive out the Jebusites

verses.text: But the descendants of Judah could not {{yarash|drive out}} the Jebusites living in Jerusalem. So to this day the Jebusites live there among the descendants of Judah.

berakhah.gloss: Blessing, a gift of favor and good. Acsah asks her father for a berakhah, and specifically for springs of water to make her dry Negev land fruitful. Her bold, believing request, and Caleb's generous granting of both upper and lower springs, is a small picture of a child asking a father for good gifts and receiving abundantly, an echo of how God delights to give good things to those who ask.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. Judah's large and detailed nachalah is received as God's gift by promise. As the royal tribe from whom the Messiah would come, Genesis 49:10, Judah is given its portion first, and its careful, city-by-city record testifies that God kept His promise to settle His people in the land in concrete, nameable fullness.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, dispossess, drive out. The chapter ends by admitting that Judah could not yarash the Jebusites from Jerusalem. The same verb that runs triumphantly through the conquest here marks a failure, an incomplete obedience that leaves a pagan stronghold in the very city where God would one day set His King, a gap the true Son of Judah would finally close.

basic.context: Judah, the tribe of the coming King, is the first to receive its inheritance, and it is the largest. The chapter carefully traces Judah's borders, then tells a vivid story: Caleb takes Hebron and drives out the giants, and offers his daughter Acsah to whoever captures the town of Debir. His nephew Othniel does it and wins Acsah, who then boldly asks her father not just for dry land but for springs of water, and Caleb generously gives her both upper and lower springs. Then comes a long list of Judah's cities in every region, south, foothills, hill country, and wilderness. But the chapter ends on a sober note: Judah could not drive the Jebusites out of Jerusalem, so they remained. The question underneath is: how does God's faithful gift of the land sit alongside His people's incomplete obedience?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, mostly a legal land record with two narrative episodes. The detailed geography testifies concretely to God's faithfulness. The Acsah story models bold, believing petition to a generous father. The closing failure to take Jerusalem is a candid admission of incomplete obedience, part of a recurring pattern in Joshua and Judges that we should not gloss over; it points forward to the need for a greater deliverer.
basic.originalLanguages: Judah receives its nachalah, its inheritance, first and largest. Acsah asks her father for a berakhah, a blessing, and receives springs in abundance. And the chapter ends admitting Judah could not yarash, drive out, the Jebusites from Jerusalem.
basic.typology: Judah as the leading, royal tribe points to Christ, the Lion of Judah, the King who comes from this line. Acsah's bold request to a generous father pictures the confidence with which God's children may ask Him for good gifts. And the unconquered Jerusalem points ahead to the true Son of David who will claim that city and secure the true inheritance.
basic.christ: Judah is the tribe of the Messiah; from it comes David and, at last, Jesus, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Revelation 5:5. The very Jerusalem that Judah could not fully take would become the city of David and the place where Christ would die and rise, so that the incompleteness here points to the completeness of what Christ accomplishes. Acsah's springs foreshadow the living water Christ freely gives.
basic.god: God is faithful, giving Judah a full and detailed inheritance just as He promised. He is generous, delighting in the bold request and answering abundantly, as pictured in Caleb's gift of springs. And He is patient and purposeful, leaving even the failure at Jerusalem within His larger plan to bring the true King from Judah.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 49 promises the scepter will not depart from Judah. Judges 1 retells Caleb, Othniel, and Acsah, and the ongoing failure to drive out the Canaanites. 2 Samuel 5 records David finally taking Jerusalem from the Jebusites. And Revelation 5 names Christ the Lion of the tribe of Judah.
basic.oneStory: Judah, the royal tribe, receives its inheritance first, a concrete testimony to God's faithfulness, and within it faith is rewarded and generosity shown. Yet the unconquered Jebusites in Jerusalem mark the incompleteness of Israel's obedience. Both the promise and the gap point forward to the Son of Judah, Jesus, who claims the city and secures the inheritance forever.

context.literary: Chapter 15 gives the fullest of all the tribal allotments, fitting for Judah's prominence. It combines a formal boundary description, verses 1 to 12, a lively narrative of Caleb, Othniel, and Acsah, verses 13 to 19, a long organized city list grouped by region, verses 20 to 62, and a closing note of incomplete conquest, verse 63. The Acsah episode, retold in Judges 1, brightens the geography with a human story of faith and blessing. The final verse about the Jebusites is deliberately placed to end the chapter on a note of unfinished business.
context.historical: Judah's territory covered the southern part of the land, from the Dead Sea and the wilderness to the Mediterranean, including the Negev, the Shephelah foothills, the central hill country, and the Judean wilderness. Hebron and Debir were hill-country strongholds associated with the Anakim. The Negev land Acsah received was dry, so springs were precious, making her request shrewd and faith-filled. Jerusalem, held by the Jebusites, sat on Judah's northern border; it would not be fully taken until David captured it and made it his capital, 2 Samuel 5:6-9. The Philistine cities on the coast, Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza, though listed, largely remained unsubdued.
context.canonical: Judah's priority among the tribes reflects the blessing of Jacob, that the scepter would not depart from Judah until the one to whom it belongs comes, Genesis 49:10, setting the stage for David and ultimately for Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Revelation 5:5. The unconquered Jebusite Jerusalem becomes significant: David will take it and make it the city of the great King, and it will be the place of the temple and, at last, of the cross and empty tomb. The recurring failure to drive out the inhabitants, honestly recorded here and expanded in Judges 1, exposes the need for a deliverer who will finish what Israel could not.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Judah's inheritance in full detail as the leading, royal tribe, testifying to God's faithfulness in giving the land, to preserve the story of Caleb and Acsah as an example of faith and generous blessing, and to note honestly that Judah failed to drive the Jebusites from Jerusalem.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The boundary and city lists describe Israel's historical settlement, not a template for us. The Acsah episode is not a rule but an illustration of bold petition and generous giving. The failure at Jerusalem is described, not endorsed; it is a candid record that exposes incomplete obedience and its consequences, pointing to the need for a greater deliverer.

debates.question: What is the significance of the note that Judah could not drive out the Jebusites?

views.label: An honest record of incomplete obedience
views.case: Scripture repeatedly, and here candidly, records that Israel did not fully drive out the Canaanites, a failure that would ensnare them in idolatry, Judges 2:1-3; the note is not neutral geography but a sober theological flag that the conquest, though real, was left incomplete by Israel's faltering faith and obedience.

views.label: A pointer forward to the true King who takes the city
views.case: The very city Judah could not take would be captured by David, the son of Judah, and made the city of the great King, 2 Samuel 5; the unfinished conquest sets up the coming of the King from Judah who claims Jerusalem, so the gap in the story is filled first by David and ultimately by Christ, the Lion of Judah.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor the verse: it exposes the real cost of incomplete obedience and, at the same time, opens the story toward the greater Son of Judah who will do what Israel could not, securing the city and the inheritance.

study.originalLanguages: Three words open up the chapter's meaning. Judah receives its nachalah, its inheritance, first and in the fullest detail of any tribe, fitting for the royal tribe from whom the scepter would not depart, Genesis 49:10; the careful, city-by-city record is God's faithfulness made concrete and nameable. In the middle stands a small, warm scene built on the word berakhah, blessing: Acsah, given dry land in the Negev, comes to her father and asks, give me a blessing, and specifically springs of water, and generous Caleb gives her both the upper and lower springs, a picture of a child boldly asking a father for good and receiving abundantly. But the chapter ends on a darker word: Judah could not yarash, drive out, the Jebusites from Jerusalem. The verb that had rung with triumph through the conquest now marks a failure, leaving a pagan fortress standing in the very city where God would one day enthrone His King. Inheritance, blessing, and a driving-out left undone: the vocabulary holds together God's faithful gift, His generous heart, and the incompleteness of His people's obedience.

typology.type: Judah, the leading and royal tribe, given its inheritance first
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the King who comes from this line, Genesis 49:9-10 and Revelation 5:5.

typology.type: Acsah's bold request to a generous father, granted springs of water
typology.fulfillment: God's children boldly asking their Father for good gifts, and Christ giving living water freely, Matthew 7:11 and John 4:14.
The city taken by David the son of Judah and made the place of the great King, and of the cross and resurrection, 2 Samuel 5:6-9 and Luke 9:31.

study.christ: This chapter belongs to the tribe of the King. Judah receives its inheritance first because from Judah comes the ruler, the scepter that will not depart until he comes to whom it belongs, Genesis 49:10, a line that runs through David to Jesus, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, who alone is worthy to open the scroll, Revelation 5:5. Two details point to Him. Acsah asks her generous father for springs of water and receives both upper and lower springs; so the Lord Jesus gives freely the living water welling up to eternal life to all who ask, and invites the thirsty to come and drink, John 4:14 and 7:37-38. And the Jerusalem that Judah could not fully take would be claimed by David, the son of Judah, and become the city where Christ was crucified and rose again, so the very gap in Israel's obedience becomes the stage for the King's greatest work. What Israel left undone, the Son of Judah completes, securing the city and the inheritance forever.
study.god: The God of this chapter is faithful, generous, and sovereign over an unfolding plan. He is faithful, giving Judah its full inheritance just as He promised, recorded down to the last village so that no one can doubt He kept His word. He is generous, pictured in Caleb's glad granting of springs to a daughter who dared to ask, a small window onto the Father who delights to give good gifts to His children who ask Him. And He is sovereign and patient, weaving even the failure at Jerusalem into His larger purpose, for from this royal tribe He will bring David and, at last, the Messiah, who will take the city and finish what Israel could not. The God who settles His people in the land is the same God who is quietly steering history toward the King from Judah and the inheritance He secures.

crossReferences.note: Jacob blesses Judah as the royal tribe; the scepter will not depart until the one to whom it belongs comes.
The story of Othniel and Acsah's springs is retold, along with Israel's continuing failure to drive out the Canaanites.
David, the son of Judah, captures Jerusalem from the Jebusites and makes it the city of David.
If earthly fathers give good gifts, how much more the Father in heaven, the reality behind Caleb's generous giving.
The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, the King to whom Judah's priority points.

study.oneStory: Judah, the royal tribe, receives its inheritance first and fullest, a concrete testimony to God's faithfulness, and within it faith is rewarded and a father's generosity shown in Acsah's springs. Yet the unconquered Jebusites in Jerusalem expose the incompleteness of Israel's obedience at the very city where God will enthrone His King. Both the promise kept and the gap left point forward to the Son of Judah, Jesus, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, who claims the city, gives the living water, and secures the inheritance forever.

quiz.q: Why does Judah receive its inheritance first and in the greatest detail?

options.text: Because Judah is the leading, royal tribe from which the Messiah would come.
options.note: Judah's priority reflects Jacob's blessing that the scepter would come from Judah, Genesis 49:10.

options.text: Because Judah had the most warriors of any tribe.
options.note: Judah was prominent, but its priority rests on its royal, Messianic calling, not merely army size.

options.text: Because Judah paid the most for its land.
options.note: No payment was involved; the inheritance was God's gift, and Judah's place is its royal role.

options.text: Because the other tribes refused their portions.
options.note: The other tribes received their portions too; Judah's priority is due to its royal calling.

quiz.q: What did Acsah ask her father Caleb for, and what did she receive?

options.text: She boldly asked for springs of water in addition to her land, and Caleb gave her both upper and lower springs.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 show her bold request and Caleb's generous answer.

options.text: She asked only for the dry Negev land and got nothing more.
options.note: She already had the land; she asked for and received the springs as well.

options.text: She demanded her father's whole estate.
options.note: She asked for a blessing of springs, not the entire estate.

options.text: She refused any gift from her father.
options.note: The opposite: she asked boldly and received generously.

quiz.q: How does the chapter honestly end regarding Jerusalem?

options.text: Judah could not drive out the Jebusites, who remained in Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 63 candidly records the incomplete conquest of Jerusalem.

options.text: Judah fully conquered Jerusalem and settled it.
options.note: The opposite: Judah could not drive out the Jebusites; Jerusalem was not yet fully taken.

options.text: Judah destroyed Jerusalem completely and left it in ruins.
options.note: Jerusalem was not destroyed; the Jebusites remained living there among Judah.

options.text: Jerusalem was given to another tribe entirely.
options.note: Jerusalem lay on Judah's border; the note is that Judah could not drive out its Jebusite inhabitants.

quiz.q: How does the unconquered Jerusalem point forward in Scripture?

options.text: David, the son of Judah, would later take it, and it would become the city of the great King and the place of Christ's death and resurrection.
options.note: The gap is filled by David in 2 Samuel 5 and ultimately by Christ, the Son of Judah.

options.text: It shows that God's plan for Jerusalem had permanently failed.
options.note: The opposite: the unfinished conquest sets up David and Christ; God's plan advanced, it did not fail.

options.text: It means Jerusalem would never matter in the Bible again.
options.note: Jerusalem becomes central to the rest of Scripture as the city of the King.

options.text: It teaches that incomplete obedience never has consequences.
options.note: The incomplete conquest did have consequences; it exposed a real failure that pointed to the need for a greater deliverer.

questions: The chapter records Judah's inheritance down to the smallest village, testifying to God's faithfulness in detail. Where do you need to remember that God keeps His promises concretely, not just in general?
Acsah boldly asked her father for more than the bare minimum and received abundantly. How does her example encourage you to ask your heavenly Father boldly for good gifts?
Judah could not drive out the Jebusites, leaving unfinished business that would cause trouble later. Where in your life is incomplete obedience leaving a stronghold that needs to be dealt with?
The very city Judah could not take became the city of the King and the place of the cross. How does it comfort you that God works even through your failures toward His good purposes?
Judah is the tribe of the Lion, Jesus. How does knowing that all these details lead to Christ deepen your reading even of a chapter full of place names?

translationNotes.note: The Jebusites, that is, Jerusalem, are named on Judah's boundary here and again in verse 63; the city would not be fully taken until David, 2 Samuel 5.

The BSB sets off Kiriath-arba, that is, Hebron with an em dash; this and the similar em dashes in verse 14 and in the city totals of verses 32, 36, 41, 44, 51, 54, 57, 59, 60, and 62 have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
The BSB footnote notes a textual variant, that some manuscripts read he urged her rather than she urged him; the parallel is Judges 1:14.
Acsah's request for springs reflects the dryness of the Negev land she received; the upper and lower springs would make it fruitful.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 15:1-63, the full Judah allotment including all boundary and town lists. Line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in verse 13, 14, and the city-total verses (32, 36, 41, 44, 51, 54, 57, 59, 60, 62) were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
